items.q: Why are tropical rainforest soils often nutrient-poor despite abundant vegetation?
items.correct: Rapid decomposition and uptake mean nutrients cycle quickly through living biomass, not soil.
items.misconception: Rainforests have rich soils because they are productive.
items.random_wrong: Rainforests are deserts most of the year.
items.why: Students assume productive = rich soil. Actually most nutrients in rainforests are in living biomass; rapid uptake leaves soils thin. That's why clear-cutting destroys long-term productivity quickly.

items.correct: Dissolved CO2 forms carbonic acid, lowering ocean pH.
items.misconception: Acid rain falling on the ocean.
items.why: Students think acidification is direct pollution. The main driver is atmospheric CO2 dissolving in seawater - an inevitable result of higher atmospheric concentrations, threatening shell-forming organisms.

items.q: Why is burning fossil fuels considered to disrupt the carbon cycle?
items.correct: It releases carbon stored for millions of years much faster than the cycle can absorb.
items.misconception: It creates new carbon that did not exist before.
items.why: Students think combustion creates carbon. It RELEASES previously stored carbon - the issue is rate. Geological storage took millions of years; we're releasing it in centuries, overwhelming natural sinks.
The answer examiners want is: Soil pH. “Predators” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: An organism's role in an ecosystem is its?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Niche. “Habitat” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many trophic levels does a typical food chain have at minimum?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 levels. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
